label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so there's this problem about 100 lockers, all starting closed. Person 1 opens every locker. Then Person 2 toggles every 2nd locker, and so on up to Person 100, who toggles every 100th locker. The question is, how many lockers are open at the end? Hmm, let me think. Each locker is toggled by people whose number is a factor of the locker number. For example, locker number 6 would be toggled by Person 1, 2, 3, and 6. Because those are the factors of 6. Each time a person toggles a locker, it switches its state from open to closed or vice versa. So, the key here is how many times each locker is toggled. If a locker is toggled an even number of times, it ends up closed because each toggle switches it, and an even number of switches would bring it back to the original state. Since all lockers start closed, an even number of toggles would mean it's closed again. But if a locker is toggled an odd number of times, it will end up open. So the problem reduces to finding how many numbers between 1 and 100 have an odd number of factors. Wait, but normally, factors come in pairs.
